question_text: Verify the address of the emergency. What is the exact address?
What is the callback number you are calling from?
Tell me exactly what happened.
How old is the patient?
Is s/he conscious?
Is s/he breathing?
Is s/he breathing normally?
Where exactly is the pain? Can you point to it?
Is the pain in the center of the chest?
Does the pain spread to the arm, jaw, neck, or back?
How would you describe the pain — is it crushing, squeezing, or pressure-like?
On a scale of 1 to 10, how severe is the pain?
When did the pain start?
Has the pain been constant or does it come and go?
Is s/he sweating more than normal or feeling clammy?
Does s/he feel nauseous or has s/he vomited?
Is s/he short of breath?
Does s/he feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Has s/he had a heart attack before?
Does s/he have a history of heart disease or angina?
Does s/he take nitroglycerin?
Has s/he taken any nitroglycerin today? How many?
Does s/he take blood thinners or aspirin?
Does s/he have a history of high blood pressure or diabetes?
Is s/he able to speak in full sentences?
What was s/he doing when the pain started?
Does the pain get worse when s/he breathes in?
Is there any swelling in the legs?
Has s/he recently had surgery or been immobile for a long time?
Does s/he use cocaine or stimulant drugs?
ECHO-level: Cardiac arrest — not conscious and not breathing
ECHO-level: Agonal breathing with chest pain
DELTA-level: Crushing substernal pain with diaphoresis and radiation to arm/jaw
condition: substernal pain AND diaphoresis AND radiation

question_text: DELTA-level: Severe difficulty breathing with chest pain
DELTA-level: Known cardiac history with new onset chest pain and altered consciousness
DELTA-level: Chest pain with syncope or near-syncope
CHARLIE-level: Substernal chest pain with cardiac history, stable vitals
condition: substernal pain AND cardiac history AND conscious

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Chest pain severity >= 7/10 with dyspnea
CHARLIE-level: Chest pain with nitroglycerin unresponsive after 3 doses
BRAVO-level: Moderate chest pain with no radiation or diaphoresis
condition: moderate pain AND no red flags

question_text: BRAVO-level: Chest pain improved after nitroglycerin
BRAVO-level: Pleuritic chest pain with recent immobility
ALPHA-level: Minor chest pain, non-cardiac features, no risk factors
condition: mild pain AND no cardiac features AND no history

question_text: ALPHA-level: Chest wall tenderness reproducible with palpation
OMEGA-level: Chronic stable chest pain, no new symptoms, physician follow-up arranged
Do not let the patient eat or drink anything.
Have the patient sit up or assume a position of comfort. Do NOT let them lie flat if short of breath.
If the patient has prescribed nitroglycerin, assist with one dose under the tongue now.
If the patient is not allergic to aspirin and can swallow, have them chew one adult aspirin (325mg) or four baby aspirin.
Loosen any tight clothing around the chest and neck.
If the patient becomes unconscious and stops breathing, begin CPR: 30 compressions then 2 breaths. Push hard and fast in the center of the chest.
If an AED is available, turn it on and follow the voice prompts.
Keep the patient calm and still. Do not let them walk or exert themselves.
Gather all medications the patient takes and have them ready for the paramedics.
Stay on the line with me. Help is on the way.
If anything changes — if s/he gets worse, becomes unconscious, or stops breathing — call us back immediately.
Do not hang up until I tell you to.
